title: Fitting a raddle on a tup
subtitle: Marking ewes that have been served, so you know when to expect lambs.
excerpt: A ram without a raddle harness is a ram you cannot track. Fitting a marking harness before tupping season tells you which ewes have been served and when. Change the crayon colour after 16 to 17 days to spot returns to service, which tells you whether the ewes held first time.
sourceNotes:
DEFRA Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Sheep (2009, Crown copyright). https://www.gov.uk/government/collections/animal-welfare-codes-of-practice
AHDB Beef and Lamb, Ram Management Guide (2022). https://ahdb.org.uk
Cobbett, Cottage Economy (1822), Project Gutenberg. Historical context on autumn ram management.

glossaryTerms.definition: A male sheep; a ram. In UK sheep farming, the tup goes in with the ewes in autumn to produce spring lambs.
The mating season for sheep. Triggered by shortening day length from August onward. The tup runs with the ewes for two oestrus cycles (34 days) to cover the flock.
A coloured paste or crayon applied to the brisket of a tup, or carried in a harness, that transfers to the rump of a ewe when she is served. Used to track which ewes have been mated and when.
A female sheep. Ewes reach sexual maturity at 6 to 8 months but are typically first mated at 18 months for a spring lambing.
The period of sexual receptivity in a ewe, lasting around 24 to 36 hours and recurring every 16 to 17 days through the breeding season.

content.text: Knowing which ewes have been served and when is what makes a lambing calendar possible. A
raddle
on the
tup
tells you each morning which ewes were served overnight. For a small flock of five to twenty ewes, a harness fitted before
tupping
is a five-minute job that pays back through a predictable lambing window.
When to fit the harness
Fit the harness the day before the tup goes in with the
ewes
. Most UK breeds are cycling from September onward; upland and hill breeds come in from August. The tup runs with the ewes for at least two
oestrus
cycles, which is 34 days minimum. If you want to tighten the lambing window further, 17 days with the tup should cover one full cycle.
Fitting the harness
Catch the tup with a
tup
-side approach using a shepherd's crook or backing him into a corner. Most rams accept handling quietly; a panicking animal will make the job harder and risk breaking the harness straps.

Slip the chest pad of the raddle harness over his front legs, sitting centrally on the brisket. Bring the shoulder straps up over the withers and buckle them firmly but not tightly at the back. The crayon block should sit flat against the brisket and make contact with the ewe's rump when he mounts. Run two fingers under each strap to check it is not cutting into the skin. A correctly fitted harness sits still during movement without rubbing.
Fit the first crayon in a soft colour such as yellow or orange. Note the date in your flock records. Ewes served in the first 16 days will carry a yellow mark on their rumps.

attrs.title: Colour sequence for detecting returns
attrs.body: On day 17 of the tupping period, change to a darker colour such as red or blue. Any ewe that was served in the first cycle and did not hold will return to service in days 16 to 18. She will acquire the new colour over her original mark. A ewe with both colours did not hold on first service. A ewe with only the first colour has held. At the end of tupping, count by colour: all red-only marks are the ewes you need to watch closely at lambing.

content.text: Daily checks during tupping

Walk the flock each morning and note new marks. Record the date alongside the ear tag number of each newly marked ewe. Lambing date is approximately 147 days from service in most breeds, with a range of 142 to 152 days. A serving logged on 1 October gives you a lambing window from 20 February to 2 March.

Check the harness every two to three days. A heavy tup working a flock of 10 or more ewes will wear through a crayon block in around a week. Carry spares. A worn-out crayon leaves no mark and the day's servings go unrecorded.
After tupping

Remove the tup from the flock at the end of the planned tupping period. Check the harness before removing: straps sometimes chafe the brisket after several weeks. Clean the harness, replace worn parts, and store it dry before the next season. Any ewes without a mark at the end of tupping either were not served or the crayon failed. Scan these ewes separately at five to six weeks after tupping to check for pregnancy.

items.symptom: No ewes marked after three to four days
items.cause: Crayon not making contact, harness fitted too loosely, or the tup is not working the ewes
items.fix: Check crayon contact by pressing the block with a thumb against a pale surface. Tighten the chest strap until the block sits flush. Observe the tup during the day to confirm he is serving; a young or inexperienced tup may need more time.
Catch the tup and refit. Both shoulder straps should be the same length, with the chest pad sitting centrally on the sternum.

items.symptom: Tup loses condition rapidly in the first two weeks
items.cause: Hard-working tup covering a large flock
items.fix: Supplement with a daily handful of rolled barley or a high-energy block accessible only to the tup in a separate feeding station. Condition scoring below 2.5 (out of 5) at the midpoint of tupping warrants concern.
